claim: Placing the search dog on the downwind side of the search area allows it to detect scent from a distance without needing physical coverage of every point, significantly extending effective search radius.
stance_target: Wind-aware SAR dog deployment for Algarve with consistent Nortada northwesterly
scope_conditions: Wind-assisted SAR dog operations in open or semi-open terrain
quoted_support: In windy environments, dogs can take advantage of the wind direction to cover more ground by scenting from a distance. For instance, placing the dog on the downwind side of the search area can allow it to catch scent cues without covering every inch of ground.

claim: Well-planned search patterns increase search efficiency by up to 40%, reducing area needed to cover while maintaining high probability of detection.
stance_target: Value of systematic search pattern planning vs. ad-hoc searching
scope_conditions: SAR operations; cited from International Journal of Disaster Risk Science
quoted_support: According to a study by the International Journal of Disaster Risk Science, well-planned search patterns can increase a team's search efficiency by up to 40%, reducing the area that needs to be covered while still maintaining a high probability of detection.
entities: International Journal of Disaster Risk Science

claim: SAR dogs trained with realistic mock scenarios (rubble, confined spaces, varied weather) have up to 50% higher success rates in real deployments than dogs with less practical experience.
stance_target: Training investment for lost dog rescue volunteers and K9 teams
quoted_support: The National Search Dog Alliance reports that search dogs trained with mock scenarios have up to a 50% higher success rate in real deployments compared to those with less practical experience.

claim: GPS collar tracking of SAR dogs during operations reduced search time by 20% in a documented Montana wilderness case by identifying already-covered sectors and enabling targeted redeployment.
stance_target: GPS tracking integration for systematic Nona search documentation
quoted_support: In a large-scale operation in Montana's wilderness, GPS tracking allowed the team to see which areas had been fully searched and re-deploy the SAR dogs to areas that had not been thoroughly covered. This method reduced the search time by 20% and ensured that no area was left unchecked.

claim: The expanding square/spiral search pattern is the most appropriate for areas with limited visibility or rough terrain, starting at a central point and working outward.
stance_target: Search pattern selection for Algarve scrubland and eucalyptus terrain
quoted_support: Expanding Square/Spiral Search: The team starts at a central point and works outward in an expanding square or spiral, ideal for areas with limited visibility or rough terrain.
